title: Minimum Samples for a Split
concept: Requiring a branch to have a minimum number of data points before splitting prevents the tree from creating hyper-specific branches to isolate single outliers.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary purpose of setting a 'max depth' on a decision tree?

options.text: To limit the maximum number of question levels, keeping the tree simple.
To guarantee that the tree always achieves 100% accuracy on its training data.
To allow the tree to grow as deep as it wants until all data points are isolated.

exercises.explain: Correct! Max depth sets a ceiling on the number of question levels, preventing overfitting by keeping the tree shallow.

cards.title: Splitting a Class
cards.body: Imagine you are a school principal. You split a large classroom of 30 students into two groups based on learning styles. But what if a group only has 2 students? Would you hire a new teacher, buy new books, and create a whole new curriculum for just those 2 kids? Probably not! You need a minimum number of students to justify splitting a class.

cards.title: What is Min Samples to Split?
cards.body: Minimum Samples for a Split is a setting that controls whether a branch of a decision tree is allowed to divide further. If the number of data points currently in that branch is smaller than your set minimum, the tree must stop splitting there and make a final prediction.

cards.title: A Voter Survey
cards.body: If you survey 1,000 citizens about their favorite food, you can confidently split them into groups (e.g. by region). But if you only have a group of 3 people from a tiny town, splitting them further (e.g., 'Opinions of 18-year-old left-handed gamers from this town') will only highlight personal quirks, not general trends.

cards.title: The Lone Spotty Dog
cards.body: A model sorts animals into 'dogs' and 'cats'. In a branch of 20 animals, there are 19 dogs and 1 cat with spots. Without a minimum sample rule, the tree splits this group to isolate the spotty cat: 'If spots -> Cat.' With a rule of Min Samples = 5, the tree is blocked from splitting a group of 1, preventing a weird rule!

cards.title: High Limits Prevent Noise
cards.body: You might think setting a high minimum split size makes the model 'dumb' because it stops asking questions. In fact, it protects the model from being fooled by 'noise' (unusual outliers that don't represent the real world) and keeps the rules solid.

cards.title: Small Split vs. Solid Split
cards.body: Compare how minimum sample settings affect tree behavior:

left.text:
Split-happy behavior.
Will create branches for tiny groups (even single outliers).
Very likely to overfit.

right.text:
Solid, robust behavior.
Only splits when there's a large group to justify a new rule.
Generalizes well.

exercises.prompt: How does setting a minimum number of samples for a split help prevent overfitting?

options.text: It stops the tree from creating new branches for tiny, hyper-specific groups of outliers.
It forces the tree to split on every single data point regardless of group size.
It allows the tree to grow infinite levels of questions.
It replaces decision questions with a curved margin line.

exercises.explain: Correct! By requiring a minimum number of samples to split, the tree will not create branches for tiny groups of outliers.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a branch contains 8 data points and the 'min samples to split' is set to 15, the tree is NOT allowed to split that branch further.
exercises.explain: True! Since 8 is less than the required minimum of 15, the branch is locked and cannot split.

exercises.sentence: To stop a tree from splitting on tiny groups, we set the minimum ___ required for a split.
exercises.explain: Setting the minimum samples restricts the tree from creating branches for groups that are too small to represent general trends.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios shows a decision tree obeying a 'minimum samples to split' rule set to 10?

options.text: A branch containing 6 points stops growing and makes a final prediction, even though its points are not all of the same class.
A branch containing 6 points splits into two smaller branches of 3 points each.
A branch containing 50 points is forbidden from splitting.

exercises.explain: Correct! Since 6 is below the threshold of 10, the tree is blocked from splitting and must stop there.

exercises.prompt: You are building a fraud detection model. In your training data, you have 10,000 transactions. The tree creates a branch for a single transaction that happened at 4:12 AM on a holiday, labeling all future transactions at that exact minute as fraud. How do you prevent this split-happy behavior?

options.text: Increase the minimum samples required for a split (e.g. to 50) so the tree can't create a new branch for a tiny group of 1 outlier.
Decrease the minimum samples required for a split to 1, to allow the model to isolate more individual cases.
Increase the max depth to 100 to make the rules even more detailed.
Remove all fraud cases from the training data so the model has nothing to split.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Increasing the minimum samples for a split forces the tree to only build rules supported by a decent amount of data. This prevents it from making rules based on single outliers. Next, let's compare pruned and unpruned trees side-by-side!
